reasoning: Three independent reasons make this finding false: (1) WRONG REACHABILITY — the notice is only rendered when shouldShowFeedbackNotice() returns true, which requires current_user_can('install_plugins') on line 42 of NoticePolicy.php. That capability is restricted to administrators, not subscribers or any lower role. The finding's claim of 'subscriber and above' is incorrect. (2) SELF-XSS ONLY — $current_user->display_name is the display name of the *same* administrator who is viewing the notice. There is no mechanism by which a low-privileged user's display_name appears in this notice. No cross-user attack vector exists. (3) WORDPRESS CORE SANITIZES display_name ON SAVE — WordPress core's wp_insert_user()/wp_update_user() in wp-includes/user.php passes display_name through sanitize_text_field(), which calls strip_tags() internally. This strips any HTML or script tags before they are persisted to the database. An attacker cannot store '' in display_name through any normal WordPress API. The unescaped output at line 64 ('' . $current_user->display_name . '') is a coding style issue (missing esc_html()) but is not exploitable in practice given the combination of admin-only gate, self-display, and WordPress-level sanitization on save.
sanitizer_notes: WordPress core sanitizes display_name with sanitize_text_field() (which calls strip_tags()) in wp_insert_user()/wp_update_user(). Additionally, the display gate requires install_plugins capability (admin-only). Additionally, the displayed display_name belongs to the current user viewing the page, not any attacker-controlled user.
reachability_correction: The notice only appears when current_user_can('install_plugins') is true (NoticePolicy.php line 42). This is an admin-only capability. Subscribers cannot trigger this notice or influence whose display_name is shown there.
